summary: benten-eval G6 (A+B+C) lands a coherent 6,158-line crate with clear module boundaries (primitives/, expr/, diag/, evaluator.rs, invariants.rs, context.rs), excellent doc coverage, well-reasoned #[allow(...)] attributes with reason= strings, and high-quality parser error messages. 137/144 benten-eval tests pass; the 6 failures are in engine-integration tests that need G7's engine wiring (documented scope). Feature-flag hygiene is correct: `diag` default-off in benten-eval, `--no-default-features` and `--features diag` both compile clean. Critical finding: `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` is NOT green at G6-close (41 errors in benten-engine from the documented RegistrationError size leak). The ledger line in the preamble claims it is green; this must be corrected or the clippy gate deferred to G7 in writing. Several minor correctness and code-quality findings follow; none block G7 dispatch.

pipeline.cargo_clippy_workspace_all_targets_deny_: FAIL — 41 errors in benten-engine lib from result_large_err + large_enum_variant; benten-eval --lib alone passes
pipeline.cargo_test_p_benten_eval_tests: 137/144 pass; 6 fail in 3 engine-integration files (read_denial, requires_enforcement, requires_property_call_time_check) — these need G7 engine wiring per implementer note

findings.claim: Preamble claims `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` is green at G6-close; it is not.
findings.detail: Running `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` fails with 41 errors — all in `crates/benten-engine/src/lib.rs` — triggered by `EngineError::Invariant(RegistrationError)` exceeding the 128-byte Result-Err heuristic (the variant carries ~360 bytes of diagnostic context per G6-C's spec). The benten-eval crate itself is clippy-clean in isolation (`cargo clippy -p benten-eval --lib -- -D warnings` passes). The G6-C implementer explicitly flagged this and recommended boxing in G7 (`EngineError::Invariant(Box<RegistrationError>)`). The ledger line in the task-brief claiming workspace-clippy is green is inaccurate; either the preamble should be corrected, the workspace-clippy gate should be formally deferred to G7 in writing (ledger + plan), or a temporary `#[allow(clippy::result_large_err)]` should be applied at the benten-engine crate level with a reason= citing the pending G7 boxing work.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one: (a) apply crate-level `#![allow(clippy::result_large_err, reason = "RegistrationError boxed in G7; see G6-C handoff")]` to benten-engine/src/lib.rs as a temporary stopgap so the workspace gate remains green through the G6→G7 handoff, OR (b) update the ledger + .addl/phase-1/00-implementation-plan.md to explicitly state clippy workspace-gate is deferred to G7. Do not proceed to R4b with the inaccurate ledger line intact.

findings.claim: Iteration-budget overrun returns `InvariantViolation::IterateNestDepth`, which is semantically wrong.
findings.detail: In `run_inner`, exceeding the per-run iteration budget surfaces `EvalError::Invariant(InvariantViolation::IterateNestDepth)`. IterateNestDepth is Invariant 8's *static* nest-depth check (enforced at registration), whereas the budget is a *runtime* cumulative-iteration cap — conceptually a different condition (closer to a runtime resource-exhaustion error). This mapping will mislead developers who read the trace and see `E_INV_ITERATE_NEST_DEPTH` fired when their subgraph's structural nesting is fine but the body ran too many times.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a dedicated `InvariantViolation::IterationBudgetExceeded` (or a separate `EvalError::IterationBudgetExceeded` variant) with its own ErrorCode. Phase-2 will replace this with full multiplicative iteration budget; locking in a distinct code now prevents a breaking change later. If a new code is too heavy for G6, at minimum add a TODO(G7) comment at evaluator.rs:152 flagging the mapping is a stopgap.

findings.claim: Two distinct `EvalError` types are both `pub`, creating a namespace-collision risk.
findings.detail: `crate::EvalError` (enum in lib.rs) is the primary public error surface. `benten_eval::expr::eval::EvalError` (struct in expr/eval.rs) is an internal evaluation error converted into `Err` by the TRANSFORM parser. Both are `pub`; external consumers of the crate who `use benten_eval::*` or reach into `expr` will see two incompatible `EvalError` types. The struct variant even has a different API shape (fields vs variants). This is latent confusion, not a bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the inner struct to `TransformEvalError` (or `ExprEvalError`), or make it `pub(crate)`. The struct is `pub(crate) fn new(...)` already, so the type itself being `pub` is mostly accidental. Tightening to `pub(crate) struct EvalError` in expr/eval.rs would be a one-line fix that removes the duplicate public name.

findings.claim: Each graph algorithm exists in two parallel implementations — one keyed by `&str` node-ids (for finalized `Subgraph`), one keyed by `usize` indices (for `SubgraphBuilder` snapshots).
findings.detail: `find_cycle` / `find_cycle_indices` and `longest_path` / `longest_path_indices` are structurally identical DFS walks differing only in the key type. ~140 lines of duplicated logic. The duplication was likely driven by borrow-checker friction — generifying over a `Key: Eq + Hash + Copy` trait bound would fix it. Not a correctness concern (both paths have matching test coverage), but a maintenance cost: a bug fix in one must be mirrored in the other, and divergence is easy to miss.
findings.suggested_fix: Flag for R4b. Generify over `trait GraphKey: Eq + Hash + Copy` and collapse the four functions to two. If the refactor hits lifetime pain, at minimum extract the DFS-coloring and post-order-topo helpers so the two cycle-detectors share their stack discipline.

findings.detail: The method body switches on the `diag` feature to call `diag::mermaid::render(self)` or return `String::new()`. But `diag/mod.rs` already provides a `cfg(not(feature = "diag"))` stub module whose `render` returns `String::new()`. The inner cfg in `to_mermaid` is therefore redundant — unconditionally calling `diag::mermaid::render(self)` would produce identical behavior across both feature states. Cosmetic.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the method body with `diag::mermaid::render(self)`, dropping the inner cfg. The stub module owns the feature-off contract; callers don't need to re-encode it.

findings.claim: Git history has a cosmetic inversion: commit `4f4bd3e` carries the G6-C commit message but ships G6-B's file set, and `b0ce570` contains G6-C's actual work.
findings.detail: Confirmed via `git show --stat`. `4f4bd3e` message says "G6-C iterative evaluator + invariants + diag" but the stat touches `expr/builtins.rs`, `expr/eval.rs`, `expr/parser.rs`, and the primitive executors (TRANSFORM/BRANCH/ITERATE/CALL) — that's G6-B. `b0ce570` touches `diag/**`, `evaluator.rs`, `invariants.rs`, which is the actual G6-C work. The implementer flagged this as cosmetic in the task brief. Not a correctness issue, but a `git blame` for `evaluator.rs` will point to `b0ce570` while the commit subject will mislead archaeologists who scan `git log`.
findings.suggested_fix: Leave as-is (rewriting published history on main is destructive). Add a note in .addl/phase-1/00-implementation-plan.md §R5-G6 handoff that the commit messages for `4f4bd3e` and `b0ce570` are inverted vs their content, to prevent future grep-for-message confusion. If the R4b post-impl review wants a cleaner history, it can be addressed via a forward-pointing NOTE commit rather than amending published hashes.

findings.claim: Three TRANSFORM grammar divergences between implementation and docs (lambdas-as-method-args only, JS-style methods alongside bare built-ins, single-quoted strings) are undocumented in the grammar reference.
findings.detail: Per implementer self-flags: (1) lambdas are admitted only as array-method arguments — the parser enforces this via the `LAMBDA_CALL_METHODS` allowlist at parser.rs:35-47, but docs/TRANSFORM-GRAMMAR.md (referenced in ParseError::grammar_doc) does not state it. (2) JS-style method calls (`$input.toLowerCase()`, `Math.min(3,7)`) are accepted alongside bare built-ins — dispatch happens in `expr/eval.rs:262-332` via the method-call arm. (3) Single-quoted strings are accepted (lex_string_single at parser.rs:662) with an inline comment stating the grammar doc admits only double-quoted. All three are defensible Phase-1 choices — the permissiveness is driven by the denylist-test fixtures needing `require('x')` and the DSL UX wanting JS-familiar method-call ergonomics. The risk is spec drift: the doc is the stable reference, and divergence silently ships.
findings.suggested_fix: Update docs/TRANSFORM-GRAMMAR.md to: (a) explicitly add lambda-as-method-argument production to the BNF with the admitted method list; (b) document method-call dispatch as a first-class production (the doc currently only covers bare calls); (c) either admit single-quoted strings in the lex rules OR narrow the lexer to reject them with a clear message. Pick one — silent permissiveness is worse than either option.

findings.claim: G6-C commit 4f4bd3e edits a test file; verify TDD policy compliance.
findings.detail: The commit replaces the red-phase `todo!()` shim in `evaluate_transform()` with a real wiring to `Evaluator::new().step(op)`. The edit is exactly the kind of surgical red-to-green transition R5 is supposed to make, and the replacement doc-comment documents the new behavior (`R5 wiring (G6-B): parse + evaluate...`). However, the test-policy guardrail says 'surgical edits only; must be documented'. The documentation lives in the function-level doc comment (good) but not in the commit body or the plan's test-edit ledger. If the plan tracks per-test edits, this one needs an entry.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to `.addl/phase-1/00-implementation-plan.md` R5-G6-C handoff ledger: "Surgical test edit: proptests_transform_deterministic.rs — evaluate_transform shim replaced with real Evaluator::step wiring; commit 4f4bd3e." Non-blocking; the edit itself is correct.

findings.claim: DSL-SPECIFICATION built-ins coverage is mostly complete, but `formatDate` is absent.
findings.detail: Counted 84 bare-name dispatch arms in `builtins.rs` spanning arith/string/array/object/coercion/formatting — comfortably over the 50+ target. JS-style method calls (`.map`, `.filter`, `.reduce`, `.sortBy`, etc.) route through `expr/eval.rs:eval_call_method`. The task brief explicitly calls out `formatDate` as an expected built-in (distinct from `now()`, which is correctly absent because it is nondeterministic). Grep for "formatDate" in expr/: zero hits. If the DSL-SPECIFICATION lists it, the omission is a gap; if not, the task-brief mention is the gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Audit docs/DSL-SPECIFICATION.md against the current built-ins table. Either add `formatDate(date_str, format_str)` (deterministic formatter over a string input, no clock access) or remove the expectation from the task brief. If added, ensure the function does NOT accept a `Date` object that could leak wall-clock — the input must be a pre-normalized string or integer.

findings.claim: ITERATE body execution and CALL callee invocation are staged for G7; the docstrings say so, but the behavior on the 'ok' edge is a silent empty-list / null placeholder.
findings.detail: ITERATE's happy-path returns `Value::List(Vec::new())` on edge `"ok"` (iterate.rs:68-72). CALL's happy-path returns `Value::Null` on edge `"ok"` (call.rs:71-75). Both docstrings flag the staging. The concern is observational: a user who writes a Phase-1 handler chaining ITERATE → TRANSFORM (or CALL → RESPOND) will see legitimately ok-edged StepResults that carry inert payloads, and may think their handler 'works' while actually not iterating. Since `engine.trace()` exposes per-step output, the user might be confused. Phase-1 exit criteria are `crud('post')` + audit-trail visualization, which per the plan don't exercise ITERATE/CALL bodies — so this isn't a correctness issue for Phase 1. But G7's engine wiring must replace these placeholders before anything that actually uses bodies ships to a user.
findings.suggested_fix: Not an action for G6 — the staging is correctly documented. Flag for G7: when engine wiring lands, ensure the trace's `outputs` field for ITERATE/CALL nodes carries a real accumulated list / callee return, and add an integration test asserting a chained `iterate(items, body).map(...)` handler produces non-empty output. If a Phase-1 user path could hit ITERATE/CALL before G7 (e.g., an R4b regression fixture), consider temporarily routing their 'ok' edge through `Err(EvalError::PrimitiveNotImplemented)` until G7 wires them.

findings.claim: Entry-node discovery (first node without incoming edges) is ambiguous when multiple nodes have no incoming edges.
findings.detail: `run_inner` picks entry via `subgraph.nodes.iter().find(|n| !has_incoming.contains_key(n.id.as_str()))`. If a subgraph has two nodes with no incoming edges (two disconnected components, or an invalid handler with no single root), the walker silently picks the first-constructed one. The DAG-ness invariant (1) doesn't forbid multiple roots; the handler-entry convention that G7 will enforce does. For Phase 1, this means a malformed subgraph with two roots runs only one of them and returns `ok`, which could mask a handler-registration bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Flag for G7: when engine registration lands, validate that exactly one node has no incoming edges (`InvariantViolation::MultipleRoots` or add it to the existing structural validator). For G6, add a debug_assert in run_inner that panics only in test builds if more than one root is found, so the current test suite catches this class of bug if it arises.

findings.claim: Trace timing uses `Instant::now()` and saturates to 1µs for zero-work primitives; this is a hidden determinism leak for replay.
findings.detail: `run_with_trace` calls `Instant::now()` before/after each step. `duration_us` is non-deterministic across runs. The R4 triage note in the code (`R4 triage: trace steps must have non-zero timing`) acknowledges the saturation hack. This means the `engine.trace(handler, input)` output is NOT deterministic — two identical inputs produce identical `outputs` but different `duration_us`. For replay-based debugging (ENGINE-SPEC §determinism-replay) and for content-hashing a trace artifact (if that ever becomes a thing), this matters. The current evaluator docstring says 'determinism is the load-bearing property' but that's about the TRANSFORM evaluator, not the trace. The inconsistency is fine as long as the trace is never included in any content hash.
findings.suggested_fix: Non-action for G6. Document in `diag/trace.rs` doc comment: "Trace timing is NOT included in any content-addressed hash; a trace artifact is an observability output, not a deterministic-replay fixture." This pre-empts anyone in Phase 2 who tries to CID a trace and finds the hash unstable.

non_findings_checked: cargo doc -p benten-eval --no-deps: pass, zero warnings (cd10953 doc-link fix verified landed)
Feature flag hygiene: `diag` default-off in benten-eval; --no-default-features and --features diag both compile

Module boundaries are coherent: primitives/ (executors), expr/ (TRANSFORM lang), diag/ (feature-gated), evaluator.rs (stack walker), invariants.rs (validation), context.rs (scope stack). No circular imports observed.

Every pub item in lib.rs has a doc comment. Spot-checked: RegistrationError fields, PrimitiveKind variants, InvariantConfig fields, Subgraph methods, SubgraphBuilder methods, Evaluator methods — all documented.

#[allow(...)] attributes all carry `reason = "..."` strings that are substantive, not boilerplate. Crate-level allows (clippy::todo removed-in-R5, result_large_err justified, too_many_lines justified for linear invariant pass) are defensible.

expect/unwrap audit: 9 call sites. 6 are `.expect("just pushed")` in cycle/longest-path DFS (safe; stack was just pushed). 2 are in tests (allowed). 1 is `.expect("just populated")` in parser peek (safe). No production panics.

Parser error messages verified high quality: specific messages per rejection class (`bitwise "&" is not in the grammar`, `arrow "=>" is only admitted as an argument of array methods`, `equality is non-associative; parenthesize`, etc.) with byte offsets pointing at first rejected token.
50+ built-ins: 84 bare-name arms + ~15 JS-method arms; coverage spans arith/string/array/object/coercion/formatting. `now()` correctly absent. `formatDate` flagged as possible gap.
Test count verified: 137/144 benten-eval tests passing at G6-close; 6 failures are in 3 engine-integration test files needing G7 wiring, consistent with preamble claim.
Test edits: 1 surgical edit in `proptests_transform_deterministic.rs` (red-phase todo! → real wiring). Documented in function-level doc comment. Per-plan ledger entry recommended (finding g6-cr-8).
Phase-2 primitives (WAIT, STREAM, SUBSCRIBE, SANDBOX) correctly routed to PrimitiveNotImplemented via dispatcher; structural validation accepts them (per phase_two_primitives_structural test file).
notes: G6 is a large, well-executed crate landing. The workspace clippy failure caused by the RegistrationError size leak (finding 1) is the only item I would call a ledger-accuracy issue — the code itself is correct and the implementer called the issue out; the problem is that the task brief preamble claims workspace clippy is green. That needs a one-line honesty fix (either crate-level allow with reason pointing at G7, or ledger update marking the gate deferred to G7). All other findings are minor and land on R4b or G7's plate. Recommend: accept G6, dispatch G7 with finding-1 fixed, findings 2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 11 on G7's todo list, and findings 4, 8, 9, 12 on R4b's triage list.
